senses.definition: Changing a political system or institution so that it becomes a republic or more like a republic.
examples: The new leaders were republicanising the old monarchy step by step.
Activists discussed republicanising the country’s political structure.

senses.definition: The belief in or support for a system of government in which a country is led by elected representatives and not by a king or queen.
examples: Her writings strongly defend republicanism as a fairer form of government.
Republicanism became popular during the revolution.

senses.definition: The ideas, policies, or supporters of the U.S. Republican Party, especially when capitalized.
examples: Modern American Republicanism has changed over the last fifty years.
Some voters have abandoned traditional Republicanism on economic issues.

senses.definition: Different forms or versions of republicanism in various places or periods.
examples: Historians compare the different republicanisms that appeared after the revolution.
The book explores classical and modern republicanisms side by side.

senses.definition: The process of making a government or institution into a republic or more like a republic.
examples: The republicanization of the state took decades to complete.
Scholars debate whether the republicanization of the empire was ever finished.

senses.definition: To make a country, system, or institution into a republic or more like a republic.
examples: The revolutionaries hoped to republicanize the entire region.
They tried to republicanize the old imperial structures.

senses.definition: Made into a republic or made more like a republic.
examples: After the war, the country was quickly republicanized.
Several institutions were republicanized under the new constitution.

senses.definition: Someone who works to turn a country or system into a republic or make it more like a republic.
examples: He was seen as the main republicanizer of the old empire.
These political leaders acted as republicanizers during the transition.

senses.definition: Changes something so that it becomes a republic or more like a republic.
examples: The new constitution republicanizes the former kingdom.
This reform republicanizes the way judges are chosen.

senses.definition: Making a government or institution into a republic or more like a republic.
examples: They were republicanizing the political system after the revolution.
Republicanizing the old empire proved more difficult than expected.

senses.definition: People who support a system of government with no king or queen, where leaders are elected.
examples: Republicans argued that the monarchy was out of date.
Irish republicans continued their campaign for a united Ireland.

senses.definition: Members or supporters of the U.S. Republican Party, especially when capitalized.
examples: Many Republicans opposed the new tax plan.
Republicans and Democrats disagreed on the policy.

senses.definition: The act of publishing something again, especially a book, article, or online content.
examples: The republication of the classic novel brought it to a new audience.
Republication of the article required the author’s permission.

senses.definition: Something that has been published again.
examples: This edition is a straightforward republication of the 1920 text.
The museum shop sells a republication of the original catalogue.

senses.definition: Books, articles, or other works that have been published again.
examples: The library owns several republications of early travel diaries.
Digital republications of classic texts are freely available.

senses.definition: Countries where the head of state is elected, not a king or queen.
examples: Many modern republics hold regular free elections.
Some republics still struggle with corruption and weak institutions.

senses.definition: To publish something again, such as a book, article, or online post.
examples: The company plans to republish the textbook with new graphics.
The magazine decided to republish the interview on its website.

senses.definition: Able or allowed to be published again.
examples: Only republishable material can be shared on partner sites.
They checked whether the research data was republishable under the license.

senses.definition: Published again, often in a new form or edition.
examples: The novel was republished with a new introduction.
Several articles were republished after being updated.

senses.definition: A person or company that publishes something again.
examples: The republisher added detailed notes to the original text.
As a republisher, the website must respect copyright law.

senses.definition: People or companies that publish works again.
examples: Online republishers must clearly credit the original source.
Many republishers focus on bringing rare books back into print.

senses.definition: Publishes something again.
examples: The newspaper republishes popular stories at the end of the year.
This website republishes reports from several news agencies.

senses.definition: Publishing something again, often with changes or in a new format.
examples: They are republishing the guide with updated maps.
Republishing old blog posts can attract new readers.

senses.definition: The act of publishing something again.
examples: The republishment of these documents has great historical value.
Legal issues delayed the republishment of the report.

senses.definition: Showing or involving rejection or denial of something.
examples: His repudative response surprised the negotiating team.
The statement had a strongly repudative tone.

senses.definition: To make something into a puddle again, or to cover something again with muddy water.
examples: Heavy rain will soon repuddle the path.
The field repuddled after the storm.

senses.definition: Able to be rejected, refused, or not accepted, especially in a legal or moral sense.
examples: The contract was found to be repudiable under local law.
Such repudiable behavior damaged his reputation.

senses.definition: To refuse to accept something or someone; to say very strongly that something is not true, fair, or right.
examples: The government publicly repudiated the accusations.
She firmly repudiated any link to the scandal.

senses.definition: To refuse to continue with an agreement, duty, or debt.
examples: The state chose to repudiate its foreign debts.
They threatened to repudiate the treaty if the terms were not changed.

senses.definition: Refused to accept or strongly denied something.
examples: The company repudiated the charges of fraud.
He repudiated his earlier statements in a new interview.

senses.definition: Refuses to accept something or strongly denies that it is true or right.
examples: The minister strongly repudiates any suggestion of corruption.
The organization repudiates violence of any kind.

senses.definition: Refusing to accept something or strongly saying that something is not true, fair, or right.
examples: She is repudiating her earlier support for the policy.
Repudiating the deal could harm the country’s reputation.

senses.definition: The act of refusing to accept something or saying very strongly that something is not true, fair, or right.
examples: There was a clear repudiation of racism in his speech.
The court heard her firm repudiation of the accusations.

senses.definition: The refusal to continue with an agreement, duty, or debt.
examples: The repudiation of the treaty shocked international partners.
Debt repudiation can damage a country’s credit rating.

senses.definition: A person who supports or practices repudiation, especially of debts or agreements.
examples: Repudiationists argued that the nation should cancel its foreign obligations.
He was known as a leading repudiationist in financial debates.

senses.definition: People who support or practice repudiation, especially of debts or agreements.
examples: The repudiationists in parliament blocked the repayment plan.
Creditors feared that repudiationists might gain power.

senses.definition: Acts of refusing to accept something or denying that it is true or right.
examples: Their repeated repudiations did little to calm public anger.
The report lists several explicit repudiations of the policy.

senses.definition: Expressing or involving repudiation or strong rejection.
examples: The speech was highly repudiative of past policies.
He adopted a repudiative attitude toward all previous agreements.

senses.definition: Someone who repudiates something, such as a claim, duty, or relationship.
examples: History has judged him as a repudiator of international law.
A contract repudiator may face serious legal consequences.

senses.definition: People who repudiate something, such as debts, agreements, or ideas.
examples: The treaty repudiators were criticized by international observers.
Creditors tried to negotiate with the debt repudiators.

senses.definition: Relating to or involving the act of repudiating a contract, duty, or relationship.
examples: The court considered his conduct to be a repudiatory breach of contract.
Repudiatory behavior can allow the other party to end the agreement.

senses.definition: To puff again, for example to blow air or smoke again.
examples: He repuffed on his cigar before answering.
The bellows repuffed and fed the fire.

senses.definition: To oppose or be against something; to be in conflict with something.
examples: Such actions repugn the basic values of the community.
The proposal repugns everything we agreed on.

senses.definition: Very unpleasant, offensive, or opposed to someone’s feelings or principles.
examples: The idea was morally repugnable to her.
Such repugnable acts shocked the entire town.

senses.definition: A strong feeling of dislike or disgust for something, especially because you think it is morally wrong.
examples: She felt deep repugnance at the idea of cheating.
The cruelty of the experiment filled him with repugnance.

senses.definition: Strong feelings of dislike or disgust for things that seem wrong or unpleasant.
examples: His various repugnances shaped his strict moral code.
Cultural repugnances can be hard to overcome.

senses.definition: Feelings or situations of strong opposition or conflict, often moral or emotional.
examples: They discussed the repugnancies between the old law and modern values.
Her inner repugnancies made the decision difficult.

senses.definition: The state of being extremely unpleasant, offensive, or in strong conflict with something.
examples: The repugnancy of the crime shocked the judges.
There is a clear repugnancy between the two legal systems.

senses.definition: Very unpleasant or offensive, often because it seems morally wrong.
examples: The idea of torture is repugnant to most people.
He found their racist comments absolutely repugnant.

senses.definition: Completely opposed to or in strong conflict with something.
examples: The new law is repugnant to the constitution.
Such actions are repugnant to our basic principles.

senses.definition: In a way that is very unpleasant, offensive, or morally wrong.
examples: They were repugnantly indifferent to human suffering.
He spoke repugnantly about minority groups.

senses.definition: The quality of being very unpleasant or morally offensive.
examples: The repugnantness of the crime shocked even experienced officers.
She could not bear the repugnantness of the conditions she saw.

senses.definition: To feel or show strong dislike or opposition toward something.
examples: She seemed to repugnate the very idea of compromise.
Many citizens repugnate such unfair laws.

senses.definition: Showing strong opposition or hostility toward something.
examples: He adopted a repugnatorial stance against the new policy.
The article takes a sharply repugnatorial tone.

senses.definition: Was opposed to something or found it extremely unpleasant or unacceptable.
examples: Her conscience repugned the idea of lying.
Their values repugned the use of violence.

senses.definition: Someone who strongly opposes or feels disgust for something.
examples: As a repugner of injustice, she spoke out against the policy.
He became known as a fierce repugner of corruption.
